claims.claim_text: MLflow provides experiment tracking and model registry capabilities for ML workflows.
claims.rationale: The upstream README explicitly lists Experiment Tracking and Model Registry as MLflow features.

claims.claim_text: MLflow was created by Databricks and open-sourced in 2018.
claims.span_hint: line 42 | Did You Know in Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: Databricks launch material identifies MLflow as a new open-source project from Databricks in 2018, but that domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: MLflow became the most popular ML experiment tracking tool because it was framework-agnostic and simple.
claims.span_hint: line 42 | Did You Know in Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: It became the most popular ML experiment tracking tool because it was framework-agnostic and simple.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Its framework-agnostic design and relatively simple setup made it approachable for many teams.
claims.rationale: The superlative ranking and causal explanation are not backed by a reliable primary source here.

claims.claim_text: MLflow worked with any Python code by adding just a few lines.
claims.span_hint: line 42 | Did You Know in Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: While competitors required specific ML frameworks or complex setups, MLflow worked with any Python code by adding just a few lines.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Compared with more opinionated setups, MLflow was designed to work with many Python ML workflows and can often be added with relatively small code changes.
claims.rationale: The upstream README supports broad compatibility and low-friction adoption, but 'any Python code' and 'just a few lines' are too absolute as written.

claims.claim_text: MLflow's Model Registry introduced the concept of model stages to machine learning.
claims.span_hint: line 99 | Did You Know after Core Components
claims.anchor_text: MLflow's Model Registry introduced the concept of "model stages" (Staging, Production, Archived) to ML.
claims.suggested_rewrite: MLflow's Model Registry uses named lifecycle stages to organize models as they move toward production.
claims.rationale: The docs support MLflow lifecycle stages, but not the historical claim that MLflow introduced the concept to the field.

claims.claim_text: MLflow Model Registry supports lifecycle management with stages and aliases.
claims.span_hint: line 99 | Did You Know after Core Components
claims.rationale: Official MLflow Model Registry docs describe stages, aliases, and lifecycle management, but mlflow.org is off-allowlist.

claims.span_hint: line 377 | Did You Know in MLflow on Kubernetes
claims.anchor_text: MLflow 2.0 introduced "serve-artifacts" mode where the tracking server proxies artifact uploads.
claims.suggested_rewrite: MLflow supports "serve-artifacts" mode, where the tracking server proxies artifact traffic for clients.
claims.rationale: The version-specific 'introduced in 2.0' claim is not correct as written; proxied artifact serving predates MLflow 2.0.

claims.claim_text: In proxied artifact mode, clients talk to MLflow rather than directly to the remote artifact store, which can simplify client-side credential and network setup.
claims.span_hint: line 377 | Did You Know in MLflow on Kubernetes
claims.rationale: Official tracking-server docs describe proxied artifact access and the client-access implications, but mlflow.org is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: MLflow's search_runs API supports programmatic run filtering via filter strings.
claims.span_hint: line 379 | Did You Know in MLflow on Kubernetes
claims.rationale: The upstream fluent API docs include filter_string examples for mlflow.search_runs().

claims.claim_text: MLflow's search_runs API can query across thousands of experiments in milliseconds.
claims.span_hint: line 379 | Did You Know in MLflow on Kubernetes
claims.anchor_text: MLflow's `search_runs()` API uses a SQL-like syntax that can query across thousands of experiments in milliseconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: MLflow's `search_runs()` API lets you filter runs programmatically with structured query expressions.
claims.rationale: The sentence adds unsupported scale and latency claims.

claims.claim_text: You can find runs such as accuracy > 0.95 and learning_rate < 0.01 instantly.
claims.span_hint: line 379 | Did You Know in MLflow on Kubernetes
claims.anchor_text: You can find "all runs with accuracy > 0.95 and learning_rate < 0.01" instantly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For example, you can filter for runs such as "accuracy > 0.95" and "learning_rate < 0.01" directly in the API.
claims.rationale: The filter example is pedagogically useful, but 'instantly' is an uncited performance promise.

claims.claim_text: Some organizations have millions of logged runs, all queryable through this single API.
claims.span_hint: line 379 | Did You Know in MLflow on Kubernetes
claims.anchor_text: Some organizations have millions of logged runs, all queryable through this single API.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams can use the same API to query large collections of logged runs.
claims.rationale: The module gives a precise large-scale usage claim without a source.

claims.claim_text: A data scientist achieved 98% accuracy and then spent a week trying to reproduce the run because the parameters were not tracked.
claims.span_hint: line 644 | section: War Story: The Lost Experiment
claims.anchor_text: *A data scientist achieved 98% accuracy. Best model ever! But they forgot which parameters produced it. They spent a week trying to reproduce it.*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *A data scientist got a strong result, but they had not recorded the parameters that produced it and later struggled to reproduce it.*
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with fake-precise outcome and duration, so the specifics should be removed.

further_reading.title: MLflow README
further_reading.why_relevant: Good high-level overview of MLflow's core capabilities and current project positioning.

notes: Shared pool entries S001-S003 are not relevant to this MLflow module, so no pool sources were used. Several otherwise straightforward claims map to needs_allowlist_expansion because official MLflow docs live on off-allowlist domains.
